Idiom (Liberian Kreyol): Monkey de wok, baboon de chop.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed bipartite clause with parallel subject-verb structure; habitual aspect marker de; contrastive proverb formula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One person does the hard work while another person takes the reward, profit, or public credit.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Somebody else is benefiting from another person’s labor.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech, politics, workplace, community disputes, humor, social commentary.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Widely recognized in Liberian proverbial speech and popular commentary on unfair labor, patronage, and unequal reward; also resonates with broader West African proverb traditions.
First Example Sentence: A no go keep quiet, because monkey de wok, baboon de chop inside dis office.
Second Example Sentence: Di market woman vex say monkey de wok, baboon de chop after oda people tek her business idea.
Third Example Sentence: Plenti youth de talk dis days say monkey de wok, baboon de chop inna politics.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbless proverb with reduplication for graduality and a result predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Small efforts, small savings, or steady progress will eventually produce a big result.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Gradual effort leads to meaningful achievement over time.
Usage Contexts: Family advice, education, saving money, personal growth, motivational speech.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Commonly used in Liberia to teach patience, discipline, and cumulative effort, especially in households, churches, and schools.
First Example Sentence: No vex say yu book small today; small small wata full bucket.
Second Example Sentence: Ma tell di girl fo save her money because small small wata full bucket.
Third Example Sentence: Even if di project hard, small small wata full bucket.

Idiom (Liberian Kreyol): Wan han no kin tie bundle.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative clause with modal kin expressing general possibility.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person cannot do everything alone; cooperation is necessary.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Teamwork is necessary to complete some tasks.
Usage Contexts: Workplace, family labor, community organizing, leadership, education.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A well-known Liberian and West African cooperative proverb reflecting communal labor, mutual aid, and shared responsibility.
First Example Sentence: Mek wi call more people, because wan han no kin tie bundle.
Second Example Sentence: Di chairman remind everybody say wan han no kin tie bundle before di cleanup start.
Third Example Sentence: A learn quick na business say wan han no kin tie bundle.

Idiom (Liberian Kreyol): Tif an tong de fight, but dem no de lef eatin.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound proverbial sentence with coordination and adversative contrast; habitual aspect repeated across both clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People who are close may quarrel, but they still need one another and should continue living or working together.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Conflict between close people does not end their interdependence.
Usage Contexts: Family conflict, marriage, friendship, politics, reconciliation discourse.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Frequently cited in Liberian discussions of kinship, neighborhood tensions, and post-conflict reconciliation; the mouth imagery makes the lesson memorable.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Liberian Kr: /tif an tɔŋ de faɪt | bʌt dɛm no de lɛf ˈitɪn/
First Example Sentence: My brothers argue bad, but tif an tong de fight, but dem no de lef eatin.
Second Example Sentence: After di committee palava, di old man cool everybody down say tif an tong de fight, but dem no de lef eatin.
Third Example Sentence: Marriage get trouble, but tif an tong de fight, but dem no de lef eatin.

Idiom (Liberian Kreyol): Wen breeze blow, fowl yai open.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Temporal subordinate clause followed by result clause; fixed proverb with body-part metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When circumstances change or pressure increases, hidden truth becomes visible.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Difficult situations reveal information that was previously hidden.
Usage Contexts: Investigations, gossip, politics, family disputes, journalism, moral commentary.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Very popular in Liberia as a compact way to say that exposure, crisis, or scrutiny uncovers secrets.
First Example Sentence: Nobody know who carry di money till audit start; wen breeze blow, fowl yai open.
Second Example Sentence: Election season de come, so wen breeze blow, fowl yai open.
Third Example Sentence: As soon as di family start ask question, wen breeze blow, fowl yai open.

Idiom (Liberian Kreyol): Chicken foot no kin hide under leaf.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative clause with modal kin; concrete image used as metaphor for exposure.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Something obvious cannot stay hidden for long, especially wrongdoing or hypocrisy.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: An obvious truth or fault will eventually be noticed.
Usage Contexts: Community gossip, accountability, parenting, politics, public criticism.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A vivid everyday-image proverb from Liberian oral speech, built from familiar market and household imagery.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Liberian Kr: /ˈtʃikɪn fut no kɪn haɪd ˈanda lif/
First Example Sentence: He lie plenty, but chicken foot no kin hide under leaf.
Second Example Sentence: Di teacher tell di class say cheating easy fo catch because chicken foot no kin hide under leaf.
Third Example Sentence: Soon or later di fraud go show, chicken foot no kin hide under leaf.

Idiom (Liberian Kreyol): Ef yu dig pit fo oda man, yu sef go fall inside.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional clause plus future-result clause; second-person generic subject; moral consequence construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person who plots harm against another may suffer the same harm or be trapped by their own scheme.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Trying to harm someone else can backfire on you.
Usage Contexts: Conflict mediation, politics, religion, ethics, family advice.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A deeply rooted moral proverb in Liberia, often used in sermons, elder advice, and public warnings against envy and sabotage.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Liberian Kr: /ɛf ju dɪɡ pɪt fo ˈoda man | ju sɛf go fal ɪnˈsaɪd/
First Example Sentence: Di woman laugh say ef yu dig pit fo oda man, yu sef go fall inside after di rumor turn back pan di liar.
Second Example Sentence: No plan bad thing fo me, because ef yu dig pit fo oda man, yu sef go fall inside.
Third Example Sentence: Grandma always warn us say ef yu dig pit fo oda man, yu sef go fall inside.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Lexicalized discourse idiom; standalone nominalized expression used as a full pragmatic utterance.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: You are outside the situation and do not know the full story, so you should not judge too quickly.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: You do not have enough inside knowledge to understand or judge this matter.
Usage Contexts: Argument, gossip, social media debate, community dispute, humor, indirect rebuke.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Documented as a distinctly Liberian proverb-like expression associated with insider knowledge and the limits of outsider judgment in communal life.
First Example Sentence: Before yu comment pan di case, kukujumuku.
Second Example Sentence: All di people online de talk, but kukujumuku because dem no know how di matter start.
Third Example Sentence: He just smile say kukujumuku when strangers begin pass judgment.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Lexicalized sentential idiom functioning as a compressed existential statement about changing fortune.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Some days go well and some days go badly; life moves in changing cycles.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Life has both good times and bad times.
Usage Contexts: Consolation, resilience talk, family advice, religious reflection, everyday setbacks.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Recorded in Liberian proverb collections as a compact cultural expression of endurance, uncertainty, and everyday realism.
First Example Sentence: Business hard dis month, but gbaysuluku.
Second Example Sentence: She lose di job today, yet she tell her children gbaysuluku and promise fo start again.
Third Example Sentence: When rain spoil di wedding plan, di auntie just laugh say gbaysuluku.

Idiom (Liberian Kreyol): Tall man an short man all de si di same sky.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated noun phrase subject with habitual predicate and universalizing focus marker all.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: No matter a person’s power, status, or size, everybody remains fundamentally equal under a greater reality.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Status differences do not change basic human equality.
Usage Contexts: Politics, religion, leadership, moral reflection, public speaking, social critique.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Appears in modern Liberian adage collections and is used to humble pride, especially in discussions of power, leadership, and social rank.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Liberian Kr: /tal man an ʃɔt man ɔl de si di seɪm skaɪ/
First Example Sentence: Di senator feel big, but tall man an short man all de si di same sky.
Second Example Sentence: Pastor remind di congregation say tall man an short man all de si di same sky.
Third Example Sentence: When promotion come, no make pride catch yu, because tall man an short man all de si di same sky.
